title: Dusty Duck

{{Quote|Duck's not meant for flying… at least, not for too long.|Neeamesh Dym|<ref name="db" />}}
The '''''Dusty Duck''''' was a [[Light freighter/Legends|light freighter]] designed and utilized by [[Neeamesh Dym]], a [[Pa'lowick/Legends|Pa'lowick]] [[Smuggler/Legends|smuggler]] residing on [[Nar Shaddaa/Legends|Nar Shaddaa]] 
during the waning [[Standard year/Legends|years]] of the [[Galactic Republic/Legends|Galactic Republic]]. The craft was notoriously unreliable, would often sputter to a halt in [[Realspace/Legends|space]], and made involuntary and abrupt descents to many barren [[Planet/Legends|worlds]]. The smuggler was assisted in his [[Pilot/Legends|piloting]] by his daughter, [[Aneesa Dym]]. When the elder Dym was murdered by a client, Aneesa became the sole owner of the craft. She took the ''Dusty Duck'' on a [[The galaxy/Legends|pan-galactic]] hunt for her father's killer.

Eventually, her quarry was killed by [[Rango Tel]], an aspiring but inexperienced [[Bounty hunter/Legends|bounty hunter]] from [[Sernpidal/Legends|Sernpidal]]. As a sign of gratitude, she gave Tel her [[Starship/Legends|ship]] and offered him her services as a pilot. He agreed, and together they set off on a search for Tel's next target, [[Kam Nale]]. Nale proved elusive, but the pair eventually tracked him to [[Mos Espa/Legends|Mos Espa]], [[Tatooine/Legends|Tatooine]]. While Dym had a set of [[DUM-series pit droid/Legends|DUM-series pit droids]] maintain the ship, Tel hunted his quarry and was killed in the process. The Pa'lowick, not knowing her friend's fate, was murdered on the same day by [[Darth Maul/Legends|Darth Maul]], a [[Dark Lord of the Sith/Legends|Sith Lord]] who had been hunting [[Queen/Legends|Queen]] [[Padmé Amidala/Legends|Amidala]] of [[Naboo/Legends|Naboo]], and had thought that the ''Dusty Duck'' was the monarch's [[Transport/Legends|transport]]. The ship would remain parked outside Mos Espa for the rest of its days.

==Characteristics==
The ''Dusty Duck'' was not a very reliable craft, and would often come to a halt in the dead of [[Realspace/Legends|space]]. It was also notoriously rough in landing, and was large for a [[Starship/Legends|ship]] of its class.<ref name="db" /> The sand-colored craft rested upon three large legs when landed.<ref name="tpm">{{Film|I}} deleted scene</ref>

==History==
===Early use===
The ''Dusty Duck'' was a custom made [[Light freighter/Legends|light freighter]] designed by a [[Pa'lowick/Legends|Pa'lowick]] [[Smuggler/Legends|smuggler]] by the name of [[Neeamesh Dym]], who resided on [[Nar Shaddaa/Legends|Nar Shaddaa]], the Smuggler's Moon. The ship constantly failed Dym, who named her for both her unreliability and for a [[Duck/Legends|waterfowl]] from the [[Chommell sector/Legends|Chommell sector]] planet of [[Naboo/Legends|Naboo]]. In [[Pilot/Legends|piloting]] his ship, Dym was assisted by his daughter, [[Aneesa Dym|Aneesa]].<ref name="db" />

Aneesa became the sole owner of the ship when a disgruntled customer stabbed her father in the back. She used the ''Dusty Duck'' to search for the being that had orphaned her, taking the craft across [[The galaxy/Legends|the galaxy]] to many remote and obscure [[Planet/Legends|worlds]].<ref name="db" /> On [[Sernpidal/Legends|Sernpidal]], the [[Armor/Legends|armored]] [[Bounty hunter/Legends|bounty hunter]] [[Rango Tel]] killed the elder Dym's murderer in a [[Cantina/Legends|cantina]] brawl,<ref name="dbrt">{{DB|character|rangotel|Tel, Rango "Tank Head"}}</ref> and the young Pa'lowick offered the [[Hunting/Legends|hunter]] her gratitude, her admiration, and her services as a pilot. She gave Tel the ''Dusty Duck'',<ref name="db" /> in addition to the [[Bounty/Legends|bounty]] she had placed on the head of her father's murderer.<ref name="dbrt" />

The inexperienced Tel chose to take a bounty on the head of crime family heir [[Kam Nale]], and, along with Dym, searched relentlessly for his quarry in the ''Dusty Duck''.<ref name="dbrt" /><ref name="db" /> Nale, who used a variety of aliases and fronts, was difficult to track,<ref name="dbrt" /> but the duo eventually located him on [[Tatooine/Legends|Tatooine]]. Dym landed the ''Dusty Duck'' just outside [[Mos Espa/Legends|Mos Espa]], and she set her newly purchased [[DUM-series pit droid/Legends|DUM-series pit droid]] about repairing the craft while Tel ventured into the [[City/Legends|city]] in his search for Nale.<ref name="db" />

===Ghost ship===
The [[Dark Lord of the Sith/Legends|Sith Lord]] [[Darth Maul/Legends|Darth Maul]], who was on Tatooine hunting for the fugitive [[Queen/Legends|Queen]] [[Padmé Amidala/Legends|Padmé Amidala]] of Naboo, had sent his [[DRK-1 Dark Eye probe droid/Legends|DRK-1 Dark Eye probe droids]] across the deserts, hoping to pinpoint the location of his quarry. One of them had spotted [[Qui-Gon Jinn/Legends|Qui-Gon Jinn]], a [[Jedi Knight/Legends|Jedi Knight]] defending Amidala, and a newly freed [[Slavery/Legends|slave]] boy, [[Anakin Skywalker/Legends|Anakin Skywalker]], departing Mos Espa and heading in the general direction of the ''Dusty Duck''. Before Maul could determine that the dilapidated [[Freighter/Legends|freighter]] was not Jinn's destination, the [[Jedi/Legends|Jedi]] destroyed the [[Probe droid/Legends|probe droid]]. Assuming that Jinn had continued on his course to the ''Dusty Duck'', Maul boarded the craft, demanding that Dym reveal the location of his target. The Pa'lowick had no idea as to what the [[Sith/Legends|Sith]] [[Lord/Legends|Lord]] was talking about, and as a result, Maul murdered her.<ref name="db" /> The same day, Kam Nale gunned down Rango Tel in a Mos Espa alleyway, leaving the ''Dusty Duck'' without a pilot or an owner.<ref name="dbrt" />

With no one to countermand their orders, the pit [[Droid/Legends|droids]] simply continued their work on the freighter. They were able to rebuild the ship to perfection, but over the years the ''Dusty Duck'' became the subject of local ghost stories and fables. It would never fly again.<ref name="db" />

==Behind the scenes==
{{Quote|When I saw that the first entry was dubbed "Dusty Duck", I looked to my wife and said, "Oh, they used my name for the ship". Imagine my shock when I realized that the judges had, in fact, used my entire entry!|[[Greg Mitchell]]|<ref name="blog" />}}
The ship eventually dubbed "''Dusty Duck''" was first shown in a scene deleted from [[George Lucas]]' [[Star Wars: Episode I The Phantom Menace|''Star Wars'': Episode I ''The Phantom Menace'']]. The craft was later elaborated on by [[Greg Mitchell]] (writing as "Hedec Ga") through the [[StarWars.com]] [[Hyperspace: The Official Star Wars Fan Club|Hyperspace]] feature, "[[What's The Story?]]." Instead of choosing a more official, numbered name for the ship, Mitchell chose to model the moniker on the recurring theme of ''[[Star Wars]]'' craft being named after avians, such as the [[Millennium Falcon/Legends|''Millennium Falcon'']], the ''[[Moldy Crow/Legends|Moldy Crow]]'', or the ''[[Ebon Hawk]]''. In addition to this, Mitchell paid homage to the running gag of ducks featuring in ''Star Wars'' material.<ref name="blog">{{Blog|hedecga|6|Dusty Duck: Behind the scenes}}</ref>

Mitchell also tied the ship's story into the pre-existing one of Rango Tel, which had been established by [[Aaron Sinner]] early in the "What's The Story?" run. In doing that, Mitchell hoped to help create a "mythos within a mythos" of "What's the Story?" entries, much like some of the other participating authors. Originally, the entry had featured Darth Maul hunting for Anakin Skywalker, the [[Chosen One/Legends|Chosen One]] of Jedi prophecy, but the text was changed during editing. In the final version, Maul is hunting for the Jedi instead. Mitchell, as a horror writer, liked the idea of adding an urban legend to the ''Star Wars'' saga, prompting him to make the ''Dusty Duck'' the subject of a ghost story within the fictional universe.<ref name="blog" /> In the fan magazine ''Star Wars Outsider'', Mitchell further fleshed out the story of the ship and the Dym family in the short story entitled ''The Smuggler's Daughter''. The [[Databank (original)|Databank]] entry would rank at number seventy-one in [[The 100 Greatest Things About Star Wars... Ever!|''The 100 Greatest Things About ''Star Wars''... Ever!'']], a list of the most popular articles in the online database, published in [[Star Wars Insider 100|''Star Wars Insider'' 100]].
